risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The subjects were randomly allocated to either of the two treatment groups." Comment: The article states that allocation was random, but it gives no additional description of how the random sequence was generated (e.g. computer, random numbers, blocks, stratification, coin toss). Under Cochrane guidance, "randomized" without further context is insufficient to confirm a truly random sequence, so the risk remains unclear.

_evidence.method_quote: The subjects were randomly allocated to either of the two treatment groups.
_evidence.method_summary: Participants were randomly allocated to two treatment groups; no sequence generation method described.

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The subjects were randomly allocated to either of the two treatment groups." Comment: The article reports random allocation but gives no description of any concealment mechanism (e.g., central allocation, sequentially numbered opaque sealed envelopes, or identical containers) that would prevent foreknowledge of the next assignment. By Cochrane criteria, absence of concealment information is insufficient to judge Low risk, and there is no explicit evidence of a predictable/open assignment process to justify High risk.

_evidence.note: The article states only that 'The subjects were randomly allocated to either of the two treatment groups.' No allocation concealment method is described.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Summary: The extracted evidence gives no blinding details: "participants_blinded": "unclear", "personnel_blinded": "unclear", and "blinding_quote" is empty. The full text describes random allocation and patient-reported outcomes (VAS, WOMAC) but does not state that participants or treating personnel were blinded, nor does it explicitly state that blinding was absent. Because the study reports subjective outcomes that could be influenced by knowledge of treatment, but there is insufficient information to confirm whether blinding was implemented or broken, the appropriate judgement is Unclear risk.

Summary: The outcome assessment was mixed, including patient-reported subjective outcomes and objective measures. The paper states: "The patient was asked to mark his/her pain" for VAS pain and "WOMAC ... was used to assess pain, stiffness and physical function levels in the subjects"; it also reports that "The two patient reported outcomes were taken before treatment, after 1 week ... and after 3 weeks." Comment: Because the patient is the assessor for VAS and WOMAC, and participants were not described as blinded, knowledge of allocation could influence these subjective measurements. Under the worst-outcome rule, these unblinded patient-reported outcomes make the overall detection-bias judgement High risk even though ROM and muscle strength are objective.

Quote: "Thirty three patients fulfilling the inclusion criteria were screened and included in study after obtaining their consent. Three patients were lost to follow-up. The study population thus had 30 adults... Scores were analyzed pre intervention, at the end of 1 week and at the end of 3 weeks in both the groups" Comment: The paper reports only total attrition (3/33; about 9.1%) and does not provide per-arm randomized vs analysed numbers, reasons for loss, or a CONSORT flow for the outcome time points. Although total loss appears low, the missing per-arm and reason data mean it is not possible to assess whether attrition was balanced or outcome-related for the analysed outcomes, so the appropriate judgement is unclear.

_evidence.notes: Only total attrition is reported: 'Thirty three patients ... Three patients were lost to follow-up. The study population thus had 30 adults'. No per-arm randomized or analyzed numbers, no CONSORT flow, and no reasons for loss are described.
